title: Genesis 20
subtitle: Abraham and Abimelech; the Second Deception

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — Abraham and Abimelek; Sarah Taken Again

poi.name: Negev / between Kadesh and Shur
poi.text: Abraham moves to the region between Kadesh (major Negev oasis) and Shur (the Egyptian border). This liminal zone between Canaan and Egypt is where the wife-sister deception recurs. Abimelech king of Gerar is a Philistine — the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the coastal plain.

poi.name: Gerar
poi.text: Gerar is the Philistine capital where both Abraham (ch.20) and Isaac (ch.26) will use the wife-sister deception. The same place tests covenant integrity twice across generations.

notes.note: MacArthur: Abraham repeats the wife-sister deception he used in Egypt (ch.12) -- the same sin in a different location. God intervenes again, this time through a dream warning to Abimelech. The pattern reveals both the depth of Abraham's fear-driven instinct and the depth of God's sovereign protection of the covenant promise in spite of it.

MacArthur: God calls Abraham a prophet in his instruction to Abimelech -- the first use of the word 'prophet' (nabi) in Scripture. The context is intercessory: Abraham's prayer will heal Abimelech's household. The prophet's role is not only proclamation but intercession. The covenant man's primary responsibility to the watching world is prayer, not deception.

heb.paragraph: Abimelek protests to God: "I have done this with a clear conscience (bĕtōm lĕbābî) and clean hands" (v.5). The phrase tom lĕbāb (integrity/wholeness of heart) is a wisdom-vocabulary term for moral sincerity — being undivided in one's intentions (cf. 1 Kgs 9:4; Ps 78:72). Abimelech's claim is that his action was without malicious intent, which God acknowledges (v.6) — a remarkable moment where the LORD validates the moral seriousness of a non-Israelite king. Divine justice distinguishes intent as well as action.

God's command to Abimelech — "restore the man's wife. He is a prophet (nābîʾ), and he will pray for you" (v.7) — is the first use of nābîʾ in Scripture, and it applies to Abraham. A nābîʾ is one who speaks for God and serves as intermediary before God — here characterised as one whose intercession is effective. The identification of Abraham as prophet (before the Sinai revelation, before Israel exists as a nation) establishes the prophetic vocation as embedded in the Abrahamic covenant itself.

Abimelech protests: "Will you destroy an innocent nation (gôy ṣaddîq)?" (v.4) — the same appeal to divine justice that Abraham made in 18:23-25. The question echoes through the narrative: God will indeed not destroy the innocent with the guilty, and will not hold Abimelech guilty for what he did in good faith. The episode is a legal case study in divine justice — examining intent, withholding judgment, and exacting restitution rather than punishment from one who acted with tom (integrity).

hist.context: The chapter's most striking theological move is God's protection of the covenant promise through a pagan king's integrity rather than Abraham's faithfulness. Abimelech acts with more integrity than the patriarch. God reveals the situation to Abimelech in a dream and tells him Abraham is a prophet — an astonishing commendation for a man who has just lied. The covenant promise is protected not by Abraham's virtue but by divine sovereignty working through unexpected channels.

refs.note: The parallel episode in Egypt — same deception, same divine intervention, same expulsion with wealth. The repetition raises questions about Abraham's character and God's patience.
Isaac repeats the same deception with the same Abimelech — the pattern runs across generations, raising questions about learned behavior within the covenant family.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The second wife-sister episode repeats the pattern of chapter 12 but with significant differences. Sarna: "This time God intervenes directly before any actual wrong occurs (v.6: 'I have kept you from sinning against me'). The divine protection of Sarah — and therefore of the covenant promise — is not dependent on Abraham's integrity but on God's own initiative. Abimelech is kept innocent by divine restraint, not by his own virtue."

Abraham is called a prophet (navi) for the first time — the only patriarch to receive this title. Sarna: "The designation of Abraham as a prophet in the context of his intercessory role is significant. Intercession is a prophetic function. The verse also explains why Abimelech must seek Abraham's prayer specifically: the covenant mediator's prayer has a power that others' prayers do not."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: God appears to Abimelech in a dream — the first divine appearance to a pagan king in Genesis. Alter: "The divine address to Abimelech is remarkable: a pagan receives a direct divine communication. God's concern for covenantal integrity overrides the ethnic or religious boundaries the reader might expect. The Judge of all the earth acts justly even toward the pagan king."

God's declaration that he kept Abimelech from touching Sarah reveals hidden divine governance of the apparent accident. Alter notes the theology: the deception that threatened Sarah's purity was secretly overruled by God before any human action. Providence works at a level below the narrative's visible events.

Calvin on divine protection of the covenant: "God watches over his people, not because of their virtues, but because of his covenant. Abraham had sinned again; yet God prevents the violation of Sarah's honour, not to spare Abraham's unworthy cowardice, but to preserve the purity of the covenant line." Grace operates around human failure.

Calvin: God suffered not a hair of Sarah's head to be touched — he watched over his covenant promise even when his servant had abandoned the watch. God's faithfulness is not contingent on the faithfulness of his instruments.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Abraham called a "prophet" (navi) observes this is the first occurrence of the term in the Bible. The note discusses the meaning of nabi — one who speaks on behalf of God, including the intercessory function. The note observes that the intercessory role of the prophet (praying for others before God) is the dominant function in this passage.

The NET note on God appearing to Abimelech in a dream observes this is the only case in Genesis where God speaks to a pagan ruler in a dream. God holds even non-covenant people accountable for unwitting moral violation.

MacArthur: Abraham's explanation -- 'there is surely no fear of God in this place' -- is proved wrong by the narrative. Abimelech, the pagan king, responds to God's warning with immediate obedience and moral indignation at Abraham's deception. The covenant patriarch misjudged the pagan king; Abimelech's moral sensitivity exceeds Abraham's in this episode.

MacArthur: Abraham prayed to God, and God healed Abimelech, his wife, and his female slaves so they could have children. The covenant man who had failed to protect his wife is restored to the intercessory role -- praying for the very family he had endangered. God's restoration of Abraham to usefulness despite his failure is an act of covenant grace.

heb.paragraph: "For the LORD had closed up every womb in Abimelech's household" (v.18 — ʿāṣōr ʿāṣar YHWH) — the doubling of the verb ("shut up every shut") expresses totality. This divine closing of wombs — one of the most intimate acts of divine sovereignty — mirrors the opening of Sarah's womb in ch.21. God controls both — the closing of Abimelech's household and the opening of the covenant woman's womb are two sides of the same divine purpose. The same verb describes Hannah's situation (1 Sam 1:5-6) until God "remembered" her.

Abimelech's generous restitution to Abraham (v.14-16: sheep, cattle, male and female slaves, silver, and land) is described in terms of full covenantal restoration. The thousand pieces of silver as "vindication" (kesut ʿênayim — "covering of the eyes") for Sarah is unique — compensation paid to publicly establish her honour. The entire episode demonstrates that the covenant promises are protected not just by divine intervention but through the moral workings of justice even among those outside the covenant community.

"Abraham prayed (wayyitpallel) to God, and God healed Abimelech" (v.17) — the first use of the reflex hitpallel ("to pray") in connection with healing. Abraham as prophet (v.7) intercedes for the very man whose household suffered because of Abraham's own deception. The intercession is effective: God heals because Abraham prays. The pattern — covenant mediator praying for the one against whom covenant sin has been committed — is the model for all subsequent prophetic-priestly mediation in Israel.

hist.context: Abraham's explanation (v.11–13) is psychologically honest and morally uncomfortable: he lied because he was afraid, and he has been doing this systematically since leaving home. Yet God still calls him a prophet and uses his prayer to heal Abimelech. Grace operates in the midst of ongoing human failure — this is not a comfortable message but a realistic one about how God works with flawed people.

refs.note: This is the second wife-sister episode (the first in Egypt, Gen 12). The repetition reveals a persistent flaw in Abraham's character: even after receiving the covenant, he defaults to deception under pressure.
Isaac repeats the same deception with Abimelek in chapter 26 — a third iteration. The pattern shows that character flaws transmit generationally; each patriarch must learn trust independently.
The psalm celebrates God's protection of the patriarchs: 'He allowed no one to oppress them; for their sake he rebuked kings.' The Abimelek episodes are the narrative basis for this hymnic claim.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abimelech's rebuke of Abraham and Abraham's explanation contain a revealing detail: "She is really my sister, the daughter of my father though not of my mother." Sarna: "Abraham's explanation reveals that the half-truth technique was premeditated — a system he and Sarah agreed upon before entering any new region. The pattern of chapter 12 was not a one-time lapse but a prepared deception. The narrative reveals the full scope of what was only partly visible in chapter 12."

Abimelech's gifts include a 'covering of the eyes' (kesut einayim) — a legal idiom for vindicating Sarah's honour before all witnesses. Sarna explains the large silver payment (1,000 shekels) is specifically designated for Sarah's vindication, not Abraham's enrichment.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abraham's explanation — "I said to myself, there is surely no fear of God in this place" — is both revealing and ironic. Alter: "Abraham assumed that Abimelech had no moral compass. He was wrong: Abimelech demonstrated more moral sensitivity in this episode than Abraham did. The assumption of moral vacuum in the pagan reveals Abraham's own moral condescension."

Abimelech's speech is addressed directly to the one most wronged — Sarah herself. Alter notes this is unusual: the wronged woman is addressed in her own right, not through her husband. The payment is her vindication, her 'covering of eyes' against any who might suspect her honour was compromised.

Abimelech's rebuke — "you have done things to me that should not be done" — is the second time in the Abraham narrative that a pagan king rebukes the patriarch (cf. Pharaoh in 12:18-19). Calvin: "God used the mouth of an unbelieving king to rebuke the sin of his servant Abraham. He is not so partial to his own people as to allow their sins to pass without correction, even when the correction comes from an unexpected quarter."

Calvin: Abraham's explanation — the place had no fear of God, she was his half-sister — does not justify his conduct but explains it as a failure of faith. The man who doubts God's protection will devise human devices that produce new dangers.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Abraham's reference to "when God had me wander from my father's household" observes the interesting use of plural verbs with God (elohim) — a grammatical feature sometimes used in the Bible with singular meaning. The note discusses whether this reflects an early polytheistic idiom that has been recontextualised or simply the plural of majesty.

The NET note on the 1,000-shekel vindication payment discusses this extravagant sum — approximately 20 times a typical annual wage — as signalling genuine alarm at having unwittingly violated the covenant patriarch's household.

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: Called a prophet by God (v.7) immediately after his most systematic moral failure. The juxtaposition is theologically jarring. God's gifts and callings are without revocation (Rom 11:29) — they rest on grace, not performance.

ppl.name: Abimelech
ppl.role: King of Gerar; acts with more integrity than Abraham
ppl.text: Receives a divine dream, acts immediately on it, rebukes Abraham justly, compensates Sarah generously. A pagan king models the "fear of God" that Abraham claimed was absent in Gerar (v.11) — but appears to be absent in Abraham himself.

ppl.name: Sarah
ppl.text: Her silence throughout the chapter is notable. She has no recorded speech. She is the most affected party — taken into a foreign king's household — yet her response is unrecorded. The text leaves her inner life poignantly hidden.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Then God said to him in the dream, 'Yes, I know you did this with a clear conscience, and so I have kept you from sinning against me. That is why I did not let you touch her.'"ESV"Yes, I know that you have done this in the integrity of your heart, and it was I who kept you from sinning against me. Therefore I did not let you touch her."KJV"Yea, I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart; for I also withheld thee from sinning against me: therefore suffered I thee not to touch her."

src.title: Ugaritic texts on divine dreams
src.quote: Dreams as divine communication are extensively attested in ANE literature (Ugarit, Mari, Mesopotamia). The Abimelech dream follows the standard ANE pattern: divine appearance at night with a direct message.
src.note: Confirms the cultural plausibility of dream-revelation in the patriarchal world.

rec.text: Origen allegorized the "sister" as the soul, and Abimelech's failure to "know" her as the world's inability to penetrate spiritual reality. His reading was influential in the mystical tradition despite being far from the literal sense.

Contemporary scholarship raises ethical questions about the text's apparent comfort with Abraham's deception. Some see the narrative as critiquing Abraham through Abimelech's words; others note the text never explicitly condemns him. The ambiguity is intentional — the reader must engage with the moral complexity.

rows.label: God Protects the Covenant Despite Covenant Failure
rows.text: God tells Abimelech: I kept you from sinning against me. The divine protection of Sarah operates below the level of human action. The covenant's security is God's, not Abraham's.

rows.label: The Pagan King Rebukes the Covenant Patriarch
rows.text: The pattern recurs: the pagan king asks questions Abraham cannot answer without shame. The outside world often sees the covenant community's failures more clearly than the community sees them itself.

hebtext.note: Abimelek protests to God: "I have done this with a clear conscience (bĕtōm lĕbābî) and clean hands" (v.5). The phrase tom lĕbāb (integrity/wholeness of heart) is a wisdom-vocabulary term for moral sincerity — being undivided in one's intentions (cf. 1 Kgs 9:4; Ps 78:72). Abimelech's claim is that his action was without malicious intent, which God acknowledges (v.6) — a remarkable moment where the LORD validates the moral seriousness of a non-Israelite king. Divine justice distinguishes intent as well as action.

God's command to Abimelech — "restore the man's wife. He is a prophet (nābîʾ), and he will pray for you" (v.7) — is the first use of nābîʾ in Scripture, and it applies to Abraham. A nābîʾ is one who speaks for God and serves as intermediary before God — here characterised as one whose intercession is effective. The identification of Abraham as prophet (before the Sinai revelation, before Israel exists as a nation) establishes the prophetic vocation as embedded in the Abrahamic covenant itself.

Abimelech protests: "Will you destroy an innocent nation (gôy ṣaddîq)?" (v.4) — the same appeal to divine justice that Abraham made in 18:23-25. The question echoes through the narrative: God will indeed not destroy the innocent with the guilty, and will not hold Abimelech guilty for what he did in good faith. The episode is a legal case study in divine justice — examining intent, withholding judgment, and exacting restitution rather than punishment from one who acted with tom (integrity).

"For the LORD had closed up every womb in Abimelech's household" (v.18 — ʿāṣōr ʿāṣar YHWH) — the doubling of the verb ("shut up every shut") expresses totality. This divine closing of wombs — one of the most intimate acts of divine sovereignty — mirrors the opening of Sarah's womb in ch.21. God controls both — the closing of Abimelech's household and the opening of the covenant woman's womb are two sides of the same divine purpose. The same verb describes Hannah's situation (1 Sam 1:5-6) until God "remembered" her.

Abimelech's generous restitution to Abraham (v.14-16: sheep, cattle, male and female slaves, silver, and land) is described in terms of full covenantal restoration. The thousand pieces of silver as "vindication" (kesut ʿênayim — "covering of the eyes") for Sarah is unique — compensation paid to publicly establish her honour. The entire episode demonstrates that the covenant promises are protected not just by divine intervention but through the moral workings of justice even among those outside the covenant community.

"Abraham prayed (wayyitpallel) to God, and God healed Abimelech" (v.17) — the first use of the reflex hitpallel ("to pray") in connection with healing. Abraham as prophet (v.7) intercedes for the very man whose household suffered because of Abraham's own deception. The intercession is effective: God heals because Abraham prays. The pattern — covenant mediator praying for the one against whom covenant sin has been committed — is the model for all subsequent prophetic-priestly mediation in Israel.

tx.title: Second wife-sister narrative — SP additions
tx.content: The SP adds minor harmonizing details to bring this account closer to the first wife-sister episode in Genesis 12. The LXX follows MT closely throughout. The Dead Sea Scrolls do not preserve this passage. Targum Onqelos renders 'she is my sister' without the half-sister qualification of v. 12.
tx.note: The SP's harmonizing impulse and the Targum's selective translation both reflect discomfort with the patriarch's deception — but neither alters the core narrative.

debate.title: The Wife-Sister Narratives (12, 20, 26): One Event or Three?

positions.name: Three separate events
positions.argument: Three separate incidents with different details (Egypt/Gerar; Abraham twice; Isaac once) involving different pharaohs/kings. Repetition of the pattern reflects the real danger of the patriarchal world for covenant women.

positions.name: Three versions of one event
positions.argument: Classic source criticism assigns the three to different documents (J=ch.12, E=ch.20, J=ch.26). They are variants of a single event preserved in different traditions.

positions.name: Literary-theological repetition
positions.argument: The repetition is deliberate literary technique: the same narrative type recurs three times to establish a pattern (God protects the covenant line through human cowardice) and to show character development (or its absence).
